paragraphs.text: The commercial usage of AI models trained off copyrighted works without the respective parties explicit consent should be forbidden. If an AI service intends to include premium features a user has to pay for, then their AI should not be trained with unauthorized copyrighted content. A company selling a video game that can be played by a single person should not require an internet connection nor be connected to a internet server to provide said features for a single player experience, including Digital Rights Managements features. Otherwise, a consumer buying said video game but only being able to play it at a later time, would not find themselves with a non-functional product in their hands. An exception to the above can be made if the video game has multiplayer features only. In this case, the consumer should be made abundantly aware that the multiplayer online service could be shut down in the future, and evaluate their purchase basing themselves off this knowledge. Any media (movies, video games, books, et cetera) that is not currently legally available for the public to purchase (for example: old console games, movies or books) should be exonerated by the enforcement of copyright laws for that specific form only, unless made available on the market again in said form or, should that not be a viable option, in a form as functionally faithful as possible to the original (i.e. minor or negligible alterations, corrections, digitalization, et cetera). In the case of video games, the code can be converted to a different and more functional language as long as most of the original behavior will be preserved. Payment processors (Visa, MasterCard, Stripe, PayPal, et cetera), due to their overly dominant and oligopolistic position with minor competition, should be forced to maintain an invariably neutral stance. Hence, they should not be allowed to decide if provide or not their services over arbitrary and personal preferences. The only exception is in case the content being dealt with is explicitly and unquestionably illegal across multiple countries (i.e.: selling illegal substances, CSAM, unlicensed weapons, et cetera). Related to the above, sex work, intended either as the release of material on online platforms or services provided in real life, should be given legal protection as long as the provider is a consenting adult and is made fully aware of what they are getting into. As a result, payment processors should not be allowed to decide if deny their services or not as long as the activity is legally authorized. Given the above, should payment processors not comply, the EU should consider providing to the citizens an alternative available for everyone, ideally released as a open source. A source of inspiration could be Pix ( https://www.bcb.gov.br/en/financialstability/pix_en | https://github.com/bacen/pix-api ) created by the Central Bank of Brazil.
